scenario: Camunda 8 TypeScript SDK worker (formerly "Node.js SDK"). Team wants to limit max concurrent jobs to 10 (e.g., to respect downstream API rate limits).
question: TypeScript SDK max concurrent jobs config?

options.text: js SDK does not limit concurrent job handling to exactly 1. The maxParallelJobs setting on JobWorkerConfig controls concurrency per worker. The default may be 1 but it is configurable to higher values like 10 for workloads where parallel job execution is safe and beneficial. Restricting to always 1 would create a bottleneck for high-throughput use cases where multiple jobs can be processed simultaneously without resource conflicts.

js SDK is configurable through the maxParallelJobs property on JobWorkerConfig. The adaptive backpressure feature automatically adjusts job activation rates based on server-side signals, but the upper concurrency bound is explicitly set by the developer. Teams can tune this value per their downstream service capacity and worker resource profile.

8 the legacy apis-tools/node-js-sdk/ is gone -- the supported package is the Camunda 8 SDK for TypeScript (apis-tools/typescript/). md:134-137 the option is maxParallelJobs: number (NOT maxParallelJobs). md:530: "Set maxParallelJobs to the maximum number of jobs you want actively processing concurrently. The worker will long-poll for up to the remaining capacity each cycle. "

a.text: Incorrect. TypeScript SDK concurrency tuning (@camunda8/sdk): Configuration per typescript/api-reference/index.md:436, 471: `ts import { Camunda8 } from "@camunda8/sdk"; const c8 = new Camunda8(); const worker = c8.createJobWorker({ type: "process-payment", maxParallelJobs: 10, timeout: 60_000, // 60s activation timeout jobHandler: async (job) => { // handler }, }); Key options per JobWorkerConfig.md: maxParallelJobs (:134-137): max concurrent in-flight jobs (e.g., 10) -- this is THE concurrency cap (replaces the old zeebe-node maxParallelJobs). timeout: activation timeout per job. maxJobsToActivate (JobActivationRequest.md:30-33): max jobs requested per activation call. Backpressure per backpressure.md:12-27: Adaptive client-side backpressure throttles SDK calls on server backpressure signals (default). Worker long-polls for up to (maxParallelJobs − active jobs) each cycle. When at the cap, no new activations are made until jobs complete. For rate-limited downstream: Set maxParallelJobs ≤ downstream rate budget. Combined with proper timeout, gives controlled throughput. Tuning examples: Conservative (slow downstream): maxParallelJobs: 5, timeout: 120000 (2 min). 5 jobs/2min = 2.5 jobs/min throughput cap. Aggressive (fast downstream): maxParallelJobs: 100, timeout: 30000` (30s). High concurrency; fast cycling. Mixed workloads (multiple workers): Different task types with different concurrency settings. Each worker independent. Beyond single-process limits: One Node process: thread-pool / event-loop limits. Horizontal scaling: multiple Node instances. Combined throughput = sum of per-instance maxParallelJobs. Per-job latency: Independent of concurrency setting. But high concurrency may saturate downstream → increased latency. Compared to Java (Spring Zeebe) (per Set 14 Q19): Similar concepts: maxParallelJobs + thread pool. Java: thread pool explicit. Node: async/await + event loop; concurrent doesn't mean parallel threads. Async-specific considerations (Node): Event loop: single-threaded; concurrency = interleaved async tasks. CPU-bound work: blocks event loop; reduces effective concurrency. For CPU-bound: use worker threads or child processes. For I/O-bound (HTTP, DB calls): event loop handles well. Monitoring: Track active jobs count. Track completion rate vs maxParallelJobs. Alert if at max for extended periods (under-provisioned). Best practices: Right-size maxParallelJobs: match downstream capacity + buffer. Activation timeout > worst-case job duration: avoid duplicate execution (per Set 13 Q10). Monitor saturation: scale horizontally if consistently at max. Test under load: validate before production. For rate limiting: Combine maxParallelJobs with explicit rate limiting in handler (token bucket etc.). Or use external rate limiter (Redis-based). maxParallelJobs alone doesn't enforce time-based rate limits.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
